FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly states 'ABCD Study collected demographic and screen use data from 11 878 children' and analyzes US ABCD baseline/annual follow-up data (N=9475, mean age 9.9 years, 2016-2018 enrollment).

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone ownership age (age of acquisition) qualifies as qualifying smartphone variable per B1. Paper reports separate coefficient for smartphone ownership as dependent variable (OR=1.41 for female, 95% CI 1.24-1.60).

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression model with odds ratios (ORs). Example: Black youth OR=3.01 (95% CI 2.50-3.64); females OR=1.41 (95% CI 1.24-1.60).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients extracted: ORs and 95% CIs reported throughout Table 2. Example: OR=1.41 (95% CI 1.24-1.60) for female sex; OR=2.78 (95% CI 2.07-3.64) for Black race/ethnicity.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study data with novel cross-sectional logistic regression model examining sociodemographic predictors of early smartphone ownership.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Pediatrics 2026; no evidence of duplicate/preprint/protocol status.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 APPLIES. All three conditions met: (a) smartphone ownership age is DEPENDENT variable in every analysis (no SM→outcome analysis); (b) every predictor is demographic-only (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, household structure, parental education, parental marital status); (c) design is cross-sectional, but B15 carve-out does not apply since there are NO cross-sectional demographics→SM analyses—the design is entirely demographic→SM cross-sectional. Paper qualifies under FT-EC7 = TRUE per binding rule.
notes: Paper is excluded from substantive analysis per FT-EC7. Smartphone ownership is treated as dependent variable throughout; no analysis of SM/smartphone as predictor of health/behavioral outcomes. All predictors are pure demographics. This is a sociodemographic-only associations study.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract states 'Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development SM Study (n = 5,639)'. Though abstract does not explicitly name ABCD, presence of ABCD SM cohort and sample size align with ABCD designation.

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying SM variables: hours spent per day on social media, platform-specific use (TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat), public/secret accounts. Qualifies under B1 (separately reported SM coefficients). Examples: TikTok b=1.52 (95% CI 1.12-1.93); Instagram b=0.92 (95% CI 0.51-1.34).

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression models examined associations between SM variables and SMA (Social Media Addiction Scale) as outcome. Coefficients reported (b and 95% CIs).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients extracted: regression β and 95% CIs reported. Examples: TikTok b=1.52 (95% CI 1.12-1.93); secret account b=2.27 (95% CI 1.65-2.89); social media hours b=0.66 (95% CI 0.61-0.71).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study cross-sectional data examining associations between SM use patterns and SMA scale scores.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in JAACAP Open 2026; no indication of duplicate/preprint status.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper examines SM (hours, platform, account settings) as INDEPENDENT variable predicting SMA outcome (regression with SMA as DV), not demographics→SM. This violates condition (a) of FT-EC7, which requires SM as DEPENDENT variable in every analysis. Per B18 rule structure, paper has SM→outcome analysis (SM variables predict SMA), disqualifying FT-EC7 applicability.
notes: Paper INCLUDED (not EC7). Analyzes associations between SM use patterns and addiction-like symptoms in ABCD youth.

FT_IC1.evidence: Study explicitly states '4,443 adolescents from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study collected at Year 2 (2018-2020) and Year 4 (2020-2022)'. Baseline enrollment 2016-2018, N>11,000 total cohort.

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure variables are 'screen time' (self-reported hours/day watching TV/videos, gaming, texting, social media, video chat) and 'physical activity' (Fitbit step count). Screen time is unspecified-platform general media use without device/platform breakdown and without separate SM coefficient. Per B5, 'unspecified-platform gaming' and generic 'internet use' without device/platform specificity do NOT qualify. No separate smartphone or SM variable reported independently. Paper does not report separate coefficients for smartphone ownership, SM platform-specific use, or qualifying SM metrics.

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted linear and Poisson regression models examining joint associations of screen time and step count with CVD outcomes. Example: high screen time (>8 h/day) associated with 4.25-point increase in diastolic BP percentile (95% CI 1.85-6.65); ARR 1.88 (95% CI 1.42-2.56) for hypertensive-range BP.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients extracted: regression coefficients (point estimates for BP percentile) and adjusted risk ratios (ARR) with 95% CIs. Examples: 4.25-point BP increase (95% CI 1.85-6.65); ARR 1.88 (95% CI 1.42-2.56).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study longitudinal data (Year 2 exposures, Year 4 outcomes) examining associations between screen time/physical activity and cardiovascular risk markers.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in BMC Public Health 2026; no indication of preprint/duplicate status.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper does NOT meet condition (a)—screen time and step count are INDEPENDENT variables predicting cardiovascular outcomes (blood pressure, cholesterol, etc.) as DEPENDENT variables. This is a screen-time→health outcome design, not demographics→SM design. FT-EC7 requires SM/smartphone as DEPENDENT variable in every analysis.
notes: Paper EXCLUDED per FT-IC2 NOT_MET. 'Screen time' is unspecified-platform general media use without separate smartphone or SM coefficients. Does not meet qualifying SM/smartphone exposure threshold per binding rules B5.
